content.text: React ships two debugging surfaces: the browser's built-in DevTools and the React-specific browser extension. Used together they let you inspect component trees, watch state change in real time, profile render performance, intercept network calls, and set breakpoints in your original TypeScript source — all without touching production code.

attrs.alt: React DevTools panel map — Components tree, Props/State inspector, Profiler flamegraph, and Console error examples
attrs.title: React DevTools panels overview

content.text: The React DevTools extension adds two panels —
Chrome / Edge: search "React Developer Tools" in the Chrome Web Store and click Add to Chrome.
Firefox: install from addons.mozilla.org — same search term.
After installing, reload any page running React. The extension icon turns from grey to coloured (blue = development, red = production/minified).

React DevTools inspects the component tree from the React runtime. In a production build the runtime is still present, but component names are often mangled and Profiler data is disabled. Always debug in development mode (
The left pane mirrors the React element tree, not the DOM. Each entry is the function or class name of a component. Selecting one shows its current props and state on the right.
Filter by component name. Useful in a tree hundreds of nodes deep.
Click the cursor icon (top-left of the Components panel) then click any DOM element. DevTools jumps to the nearest owning React component.
Right-click a component in the tree → "Scroll into view" highlights the real DOM node.
icon in the right panel opens the component's source file in the Sources tab (requires source maps).
The right panel shows three sections when a component is selected:
Every prop passed from the parent, including children
Yes — edit inline to re-render with new values
Each hook in call order: useState, useReducer, useContext, useRef, useMemo, useCallback, and custom hooks
The parent component chain that instantiated this component

To edit a prop or state value in place: click the value, type a new one, and press Enter. React re-renders with the overridden value immediately. This is useful for testing edge cases (empty arrays, null users, error states) without changing source code.
The refresh icon in the Components panel triggers a forceUpdate on the selected class component, or schedules a dummy state dispatch on function components.
Right-click a component → "Log this component" prints the instance to the Console as
After selecting any component in the Components panel, the Console exposes
as a reference to it — analogous to
for DOM nodes in the Elements panel.
The Profiler records which components rendered, how long each took, and why. It only works in development builds — the production build strips the profiling APIs to reduce bundle size.
Click the blue record circle. The icon turns red.
Interact with your app: click a button, type in an input, navigate a route.
Click the red circle again to stop. The Profiler renders a flame graph.
Each bar represents a component. Width = render time. Color encodes relative cost:
: did not render this commit (React skipped it — good).
: rendered, fast relative to the slowest component in this commit.
Click a bar to select that commit and see a "Why did this render?" section. Common reasons:
: the parent passed a new reference or value.
: useState / useReducer / useContext returned a new value.
: the component is not memoized, so it renders whenever its parent does.

Switch from "Flamegraph" to "Ranked" in the top-right dropdown. This sorts every component that rendered in a single commit by descending render time — the slowest is always at the top. Use it to find the one component that accounts for 80% of a slow interaction.
"Record why each component rendered while profiling"
(gear icon in the Profiler panel). This adds a small runtime cost but reveals exact prop and hook diffs, which is essential for tracking down unnecessary re-renders.
React writes development warnings directly to the Console. Understanding them speeds debugging considerably.

Warning: Each child in a list should have a unique "key" prop.
  Check the render method of `ProductList`.
    at ProductList (http://localhost:5173/src/ProductList.tsx:18:5)
The file path at the bottom is a source-map link. Clicking it opens the original
file in the Sources panel at the correct line — no manual searching.

TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'map')
  at ProductList (ProductList.tsx:22:17)
This is a runtime error, not a React warning. The component received
where it expected an array. Check the prop or state that flows into line 22.
Error Boundaries and the Error Overlay
Vite and Create React App both show a full-screen error overlay during development when an unhandled render error occurs. Dismiss it with
to see the underlying UI, or click the stack frames to jump to source. The overlay disappears automatically when you fix the error and hot-reload.
Vite generates inline source maps in development. This means the Sources panel shows your original
. Click a line number to set a breakpoint — execution pauses there, and you can inspect local variables, step through lines, and view the call stack.
Open Sources (Cmd+P → type a filename to jump directly).
Click the line number gutter next to the code you want to pause at. A blue marker appears.
Trigger the code path (click the button, submit the form, etc.).
Execution pauses. Hover any variable to see its current value. Use the step controls at the top-right to step over / into / out of functions.
Right-click a line number → "Add conditional breakpoint". Enter an expression like
. Execution only pauses when the condition is true — critical when a function is called many times but only breaks on a rare edge case.
Right-click → "Add logpoint". Enter a log message with
). The value prints to the Console every time execution passes that line — like
or Axios call your React component makes appears in the Network panel. Key workflow:
Open Network before interacting (requests before DevTools opened aren't captured).
Filter by "Fetch/XHR" to hide asset noise and see only API calls.
Click a request → "Headers" to see the URL, method, status code, and Authorization header.
"Preview" tab shows the parsed JSON — expand objects without copy-pasting.
"Response" tab shows the raw text body, useful when the server sends an unexpected content type.
Right-click a request → "Copy as fetch" to reproduce the exact request in the Console.
The throttle dropdown ("No throttling" by default) lets you simulate Slow 3G or Fast 3G. Run your app under throttling to find:
Loading states that flash too briefly on fast connections.
Missing skeleton / spinner UI during long waits.
Elements Panel: DOM vs Component Tree
The Elements panel shows the live DOM React rendered — not the component tree. Use it to:
Verify the correct CSS class is applied to an element.
Inspect computed styles when Tailwind class names collapse multiple rules.
Check that ARIA attributes are present on interactive elements.
Temporarily edit HTML or styles to prototype a visual fix before writing code.
To find which component owns a given DOM node: right-click the DOM node in Elements → "Store as global variable" → switch to Components panel → search for
using the cursor icon. Alternatively, install the React DevTools extension and right-click a DOM node → the context menu gains an "Inspect React component" entry.
Workflow 1 — Finding Which State Change Caused a Re-render
Enable "Record why each component rendered" in Profiler settings.
Record while performing the interaction that feels slow.
Click the commit bar corresponding to the slow frame (amber bar in the timeline).
Select each component in the flame graph. The right panel shows the exact hook or prop that changed.
If the reason is "The parent component rendered" and nothing actually changed, the component is a candidate for
Workflow 2 — Tracing a Prop Back to Its Origin
Select the component that receives the unexpected prop value in the Components panel.
Look at the "rendered by" chain in the right panel — click each entry to walk up the tree.
At each level, check the props section to see when the value changes from correct to wrong.
When the correct level is found, click
to open the component source and set a breakpoint at the state or computation that produces the prop.
Workflow 3 — Debugging a Failing API Call
Open Network, filter "Fetch/XHR", trigger the interaction.
Find the red (failed) or 4xx/5xx request. Click it.
"Headers" → verify the URL is exactly correct, the method is right, Authorization is present.
"Response" → read the server's error body (often a JSON error message explaining what's wrong).
Right-click → "Copy as fetch" → paste in Console → modify and resend without reloading the page.
Flash borders around every component that renders — instant visual indicator of unnecessary renders
Shows exact prop/hook diffs per render
Filter out library internals (Router internals, Provider wrappers) to see only your components
Keep Console output across page reloads — useful when a crash causes a reload and you'd otherwise lose the error
Forces every asset and API request to be re-fetched — prevents stale responses masking bugs
Profiling is disabled in production builds. Start the dev server (
) and profile there. If you genuinely need production profiles, add
as an alias in your bundler config.
Component names show as Anonymous or _c:
Name your components explicitly with named function expressions. Avoid default-exporting anonymous arrow functions:
Vite generates inline source maps in development. If you've customized
without mode guards, the dev server may inherit that setting. Check that
block, not at the top level.
"You are running React in production mode" warning:
even during local dev. Check your
Extensions panel empty after StrictMode double-invoke:
React 18 StrictMode mounts components twice in development to detect side effects. The Profiler records both invocations; the first (discarded) render appears grey. This is expected behavior.
Find which component owns a DOM node
Enable "Record why" setting → select commit → select component
Filter XHR → click request → Response tab
When a component consumes a React context via
, the Components panel lists it under the hooks section as
with the current value expanded. This lets you verify the context is providing the expected shape without adding temporary
Expand "Context" in the hooks list. Verify the value matches what the Provider is meant to supply.
Walk up the tree via "rendered by" to find the nearest Provider. Select it to inspect its
If the value prop is wrong, trace it to the state or prop that feeds the Provider.
Named contexts show a better label in the hooks list. Instead of
entry — essential when a component consumes multiple contexts.
console.log vs Breakpoints: When to Use Each
Both are valid tools. The choice depends on what you're investigating.
Track a value across many renders
Console accumulates every call; breakpoints would interrupt every time
Inspect all local variables at a specific moment
Pause and inspect entire scope without predicting which variables to log
Log only when condition is true
No source change needed; easier to remove after debugging
Sources panel shows async stack trace; console.log loses the async context
Live view updates in real time without adding any code
Performance Anti-Patterns Visible in the Profiler
The Profiler reveals specific patterns that indicate performance problems before they need custom instrumentation.
If every commit shows the entire tree re-rendering, a context value is changing reference on each render. Look for a Provider whose
prop is an object literal created inline:
The Profiler will show the context consumers in amber on every commit before the fix, and grey (skipped) after.

Switch to the Ranked view and sort by "Self duration" (time spent in the component itself, excluding children). If a list item component appears with high self duration and renders count matching the list length, the render function is doing expensive work on each item. Move the computation to
or lift it outside the render cycle entirely.

The commit timeline in the Profiler header shows one bar per React commit (one render pass). If a single user interaction produces 10+ commits, the component is scheduling multiple state updates in separate calls instead of batching them. React 18 automatic batching handles most cases in event handlers, but manual
code outside event handlers may still batch poorly — wrap with
only when needed (it opts out of batching).

React 18's StrictMode intentionally mounts, unmounts, and re-mounts every component in development to detect side effects that are not idempotent. This can cause confusing behavior in the Profiler and Console:
The Profiler shows two mount commits on initial load. The first is grey (discarded); only the second persists.
Console logs appear twice per render. This is intentional — if your log appears once instead of twice, a side effect is being run conditionally.
API calls appear to fire twice. The correct fix is an effect cleanup function (returning a teardown from
Strict Mode's double-invoke does not happen in production. It only affects the development build — so a double API call in development is a code issue to fix, not a production bug.

The React DevTools extension adds the Components and Profiler panels on top of the browser's built-in Console, Sources, Network, and Elements tools. Together they cover the full debugging surface: inspecting component state live, recording render performance, tracing slow commits to their source, pausing at breakpoints in TypeScript source via source maps, and diagnosing failing API calls — all in development mode where the React runtime emits full warnings and names.
